title: CMMC practice implementation evidence collection
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the CMMC programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with CMMC control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Practice implementation evidence collection supports audit reporting.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that CMMC AU.L2-3.3.5 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.32.18.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.32.18' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'CMMC practice implementation evidence collection' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper CMMC control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the CMMC control objective.

dataSources: Cloud audit trails, Windows Security Event Logs, vulnerability scan outputs, access control logs, encryption status reports, continuous monitoring feeds, incident response logs, privacy management platforms, law enforcement system logs
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for AWS (1876), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (3110), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621) (Splunkbase)
spl:
index=grc sourcetype="cmmc:ssp" practice_id="AC.L2-*" earliest=-365d
| stats latest(evidence_status) as es by practice_id
| where es!="complete"
description: Supports CMMC compliance evidence for cmmc practice implementation evidence collection in Splunk dashboards and scheduled reports.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for CMMC compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes grc and sourcetypes cmmc:ssp. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Maintain `amer_authorization_boundary.csv` with ATO, CUI, and CJIS scope flags. (2) Integrate POA&M exports via scheduled output and lookup refresh for ISSM workflows. (3) Separate LGPD and federal datasets with index- and role-level controls. (4) Quarterly control mapping workshop with ISSM, ISO, and privacy counsel. Indexes required: `index=grc`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=cmmc:ssp`.
visualization: Stacked bar (control family), Table (users and events), Single value (open findings). Key fields: `es`, `practice_id`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t latest(All_Changes.status) as agg_value from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object_category, All_Changes.user | sort - agg_value
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for Change (CIM data model).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `grc` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `AU.L2-3.3.5`. The control objective is: "Evidence that CMMC AU.L2-3.3.5 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.32.18." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `grc` under `cmmc:ssp`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Maintain `amer_authorization_boundary.csv` with ATO, CUI, and CJIS scope flags. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 2:** Integrate POA&M exports via scheduled output and lookup refresh for ISSM workflows. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

• **Hint 3:** Separate LGPD and federal datasets with index- and role-level controls. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Quarterly control mapping workshop with ISSM, ISO, and privacy counsel. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

This search aggregates `latest()` across the last year, grouped by `practice_id`. For "CMMC practice implementation evidence collection," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of CMMC control activity. `earliest()` and `latest()` extract the first and last value per group — typically used to measure durations ('first seen' to 'last seen') or to pick the freshest attribute value when multiple events update the same entity.

Why aggregate at search time rather than rely on raw events: compliance reporting requires summarisation to be defensible and stable. A raw-event dashboard can show different counts depending on the exact second it was queried; an aggregated result rounded to the UC's schedule produces consistent numbers across the reporting period. If the downstream consumer (GRC platform, regulator submission) expects stable daily/weekly figures, schedule this search to write to a summary index and read from the summary for reports — that way the numbers quoted on Monday match what was quoted on Wednesday.

Cardinality vigilance: if `practice_id` has very high cardinality (e.g. grouping by a free-text `user_agent` or `url` field), the result set explodes and the search becomes slow. Check cardinality with `| stats dc(practice_id) as cardinality` before scheduling; if >100000 distinct groups, either narrow the search, pre-filter to the top N values, or redesign the search to aggregate at a coarser grain.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Change` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t latest(All_Changes.status) as agg_value from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object_category, All_Changes.user | sort - agg_value
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Change` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.32.18: CMMC practice implementation evidence collection]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -365d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.32.18,reg=CMMC
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=grc sourcetype=cmmc:ssp earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `es`, `practice_id`. Run:

```spl
index=grc sourcetype=cmmc:ssp earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table es, practice_id
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.32.18: CMMC evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Stacked Bar (`control family`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Drill-through table (`users and events`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (`open findings`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'CMMC current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**CMMC-specific failure modes:**

• Level 1/2/3 distinction: CMMC 2.0 has three levels (Foundational/Advanced/Expert). Detections must specify level — Level 1 is self-assessed against basic cyber hygiene; Level 3 requires NIST SP 800-172 enhanced controls plus triennial assessment.

• FCI vs CUI boundary: CMMC applies only to systems handling Federal Contract Information (FCI) or Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI). Detections scoped to 'entire organisation' fire on out-of-boundary systems, wasting audit effort.

• POA&M acceptance limitation: unlike FISMA, CMMC limits POA&M use — certain controls cannot have open POA&M items at assessment. Detections must know which controls can have open POA&M and which cannot.

• C3PAO assessment cadence: Level 2+ requires third-party assessment every 3 years. Detections measuring control stability must span the full triennial cycle; short-window detections miss drift between assessments.
grandmaExplanation: We help you show privacy and sector rules across the Americas with records people can read without deep technical jargon.
